finding: duration_source classifies started_at provenance as `claimed` (from `tp claim`/`tp next`/`tp next --brief`) or `implicit` (from `tp done`'s implicit claim). But `tp commit` ALSO performs an implicit claim — when the task is open it sets status->wip and writes started_at (commit.go:66-82). A task claimed by `tp commit` then closed by `tp done` has started_at from neither of the two enumerated sources, so its duration_source is undefined. Under `builtin` strategy `tp commit` + `tp done` is a documented close path (CLAUDE.md: 'under builtin, close through tp commit ... or tp done --auto-commit'), so this is not an edge case: every task whose first claim came from `tp commit` is unclassified, and tp report's implicit-duration exclusion becomes non-deterministic for those rows.
suggestion: Add `tp commit` to the `claimed` set (it is an explicit tp command that claims, distinct from `tp done`'s transparent implicit claim), or define a third source. State which.

finding: The prior-round section's file-changed-since flag is 'true when a commit touching that file landed after the round's recorded_at (read from git),' but the spec never defines what recorded_at is or how it is obtained from git. Round files live in .tp-review/ and may be committed long after the round was actually recorded (batched/doc commits), so a git-derived timestamp (e.g. the commit time of the round file) is unreliable; if recorded_at is intended as a new stored field stamped at --record time, the spec does not say so. An implementer cannot build the changed-since flag — nor satisfy test #11 ('whether the file changed since') — without inventing the data source and its semantics.
suggestion: Define recorded_at explicitly: either a timestamp field written by `tp audit --record` at recording time, or name the exact git operation and accept its limitation; state which round (the immediately previous recorded round) supplies it.

finding: §10.2 defines the audit prior-round changed-since flag as 'true when a commit touching that file landed after the round's recorded_at', but the spec never establishes recorded_at as a field of a recorded round, and §10.9 says rounds recorded before this release still produce a prior-round section. A tester reading only the spec cannot confirm whether a legacy prior round carries recorded_at, so the flag's value (or omission) for a round-2 audit whose prior round is legacy is unspecified — two engineers could write contradictory assertions.
suggestion: State that recorded_at is a guaranteed field of every recorded round (including legacy ones), or specify the flag's behavior when recorded_at is absent (e.g. omit the flag, treating it like a file-less row).

finding: The <role> slot in file-<role>-<slug> is never defined. The slug derivation is fully specified (lowercase, non-alphanumeric to single -, trim, 40-char cap, collision -2/-3), but the spec never states what occupies <role>: the role's id/file-stem, the built-in id, or the abbreviation prefix the current code uses (idPrefix file-sec-/file-maint-, audit_roles.go:54-55). The example file-go-safety-2 implies the full role id, but this is an undocumented behavior change from the current abbreviation-based prefix. The round-1 finding explicitly asked to state which it is. It is load-bearing for both the (role,item_id) cross-round dedup this change exists to repair AND the §10.9 legacy-id detection, which must strip the same role prefix to tell a bare positional digit from a slug; if generation and detection disagree on what <role> is, dedup and legacy classification both break.

Legacy detection fires on 'an item id matching the positional scheme `file-<role>-<digits>` (a trailing run of digits with no slug separator)'. But §10.3 defines no separator character distinct from `-`: the new slug scheme itself replaces non-alphanumeric runs with `-` and appends `-2`/`-3` collision suffixes, all using `-`. So every new id also ends in a structure of `<tokens>-<digits>`, and 'no slug separator' is circular — there is no token that distinguishes a positional trailing index (legacy) from a slug-derived trailing digit run (new). `file-go-safety-12` is genuinely ambiguous under the prose as written.
suggestion: Define legacy detection operationally as a regex anchored on the ENTIRE remainder after `file-<role>-` being all digits, e.g. `the whole suffix after file-<role>- matches ^\d+$`; state that a suffix containing any letter or multi-segment slug is current-scheme.

finding: The implicit_duration and excluded_from_accuracy counts overlap with no stated precedence. An implicit-duration task has near-zero duration (started_at set just before close per §11.1), so it rounds to zero and satisfies BOTH predicates: the implicit_duration rule (duration_source==implicit) and the excluded_from_accuracy rule ('tasks whose computed duration rounds to zero'). The revision named the two fields and says 'both counts are reported', but never states whether excluded_from_accuracy excludes already-counted implicit tasks, so an implementer cannot tell if a near-zero implicit task is single-counted under implicit_duration or also double-counted under excluded_from_accuracy. This is the exact overlap/precedence gap the round-1 finding flagged; field names were added but the disjointness (or explicit precedence: implicit checked first) was not pinned.

Each prior-work entry carries the full commit_files list (up to 50 paths/task per §6.4) across up to 12 entries (§5.4), so the default brief can emit ~600 repo-relative file paths with no brief-level path budget. These are NEW bytes (the pre-v0.30 implementation unit carried no prior file lists at all — §1 table), so at scale the 'brief' is larger than the task record it replaces, inverting §12.1's premise and violating §2.6. --compact is the only relief and it drops EVERY file list (all-or-nothing), leaving no bounded middle ground for a unit that legitimately wants its immediate dependency's files.
suggestion: Cap total file paths emitted across the prior-work section (e.g. <=50, mirroring §5.4's own entry-truncation pattern) with an '...and N more omitted' note; keep --compact as the zero-files option so the default brief is bounded without forcing the all-or-nothing trade.

finding: §7.3 makes the audit phase read 'a task's stored commit_files' for file selection, falling back to deriving paths from commit_shas when the field is absent. It does not state whether this replaces or supplements the existing `tp audit` git-diff auto-detection (CLAUDE.md: 'auto-detects changed files via git diff'), nor what file source applies to a task that has neither commit_files nor commit_shas — e.g. a `--covered-by` close, which records none per §6.1. Audit is spec-scoped (`tp audit <spec>`), yet §7.3 reads 'a task's' commit_files, so the implementer must also invent how a spec-level audit aggregates per-task commit_files. The build target is underdetermined.
suggestion: State whether the git-diff auto-detection is retained as a further fallback, define the file source for tasks with no commit data (e.g. covered-by tasks inherit their covering task's files), and specify the spec-level→per-task aggregation.

finding: The reading budget is 'a per-role byte threshold (default 12 KB)' but does not say whether 12 KB is 12000 bytes (decimal) or 12288 bytes (binary). A test asserting the default value, or a boundary test with a file sized exactly at the threshold, cannot be written deterministically.
suggestion: Pin the unit explicitly, e.g. '12 KiB (12288 bytes)' or '12000 bytes'.

finding: excluded_from_accuracy is 'tasks whose computed duration rounds to zero', but neither the rounding unit nor the rule is specified (round vs truncate; nearest minute, second, or integer). A task of e.g. 0.4 minutes may or may not be counted, so the count is not deterministically testable.
suggestion: State the precision, e.g. 'computed duration in minutes, rounded to the nearest whole minute, that equals 0'.

finding: tp report gains two counts: implicit_duration and excluded_from_accuracy ('tasks whose computed duration rounds to zero'). An implicit-duration task's started_at is set moments before close, so its computed duration also rounds to zero and literally satisfies the excluded_from_accuracy definition — it could land in both buckets. The spec implies the buckets are disjoint ('an unmeasurable task is never confused with an instantaneous one') but never states that implicit tasks are excluded from excluded_from_accuracy, so the implementer cannot determine set membership or avoid double-counting.
suggestion: State explicitly that the two counts are disjoint: implicit-duration tasks are counted only in implicit_duration and excluded from excluded_from_accuracy.
